steps.action: Irish stepdance is a style of performance dance with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: Irish stepdance is a style of performance dance with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: It may be generally characterized by a stiff upp
steps.text: It may be generally characterized by a stiff upper body and fast and precise movements of the feet, and can be performed solo or in groups.

steps.action: Aside from public dance performances, there are
steps.text: Aside from public dance performances, there are also stepdance competitions all over the world.

steps.action: In Irish dance culture, a Feis is a traditional
steps.text: In Irish dance culture, a Feis is a traditional Gaelic arts and culture festival.

steps.action: Additionally, poodle socks are worn by female da
steps.text: Additionally, poodle socks are worn by female dancers while males wear plain black socks.

steps.action: This hyper-stylization originated in the Irish d
steps.text: This hyper-stylization originated in the Irish diaspora, while traditional costume was homemade or from the local dressmaker and embroidered with Celtic knots and designs.

steps.action: Riverdance, an Irish step-dancing performance in
steps.text: Riverdance, an Irish step-dancing performance in the 1994 Eurovision Song Contest that later became a hugely successful theatrical production, greatly contributed to its popularity.

steps.action: Once Riverdance became a large production, it ch
steps.text: Once Riverdance became a large production, it changed the way that Irish dance was performed and viewed.

steps.action: This meant adding a theatrical flair to the perf
steps.text: This meant adding a theatrical flair to the performance, including arm movements (as opposed to the previously rigid top half that dancers maintained) as well as sexualizing the dance and the costumes.

steps.action: Following after Riverdance was Lord of the Dance
steps.text: Following after Riverdance was Lord of the Dance and many other theatrical productions based on Irish stepdance; Michael Flatley, an Irish stepdancer, became a well-known name within these shows.

steps.action: There are different dances specific to each type
steps.text: There are different dances specific to each type of shoe, and different types of music with varying beats are played based on the dance, although soft and hard shoe dances all share basic moves and rhythms.

steps.action: Its first roots may have been in Pre-Christian I
steps.text: Its first roots may have been in Pre-Christian Ireland, but Irish dance was also partially influenced by dance forms on the Continent, especially the quadrille dances.

steps.action: Traveling dancing masters taught all over Irelan
steps.text: Traveling dancing masters taught all over Ireland beginning around the 1750s and continuing as late as the early 1900s.

steps.action: By the late 19th century, at least three related
steps.text: By the late 19th century, at least three related styles of step dance had developed in Ireland.

steps.action: The style practised in Munster saw dancers on th
steps.text: The style practised in Munster saw dancers on the balls of their feet, using intricate percussive techniques to create complex rhythm.

steps.action: On the other hand, a tradition developed in Ulst
steps.text: On the other hand, a tradition developed in Ulster saw dancers instead using their heel to create a persistent drumming effect, and primarily performing in pairs.

steps.action: The Connemara style, later described as sean-nós
steps.text: The Connemara style, later described as sean-nós dance, combined heel and ball movements with the swaying of the torso and vigorous movement of the arms.

steps.action: The foundation of the Gaelic League in 1893, an
steps.text: The foundation of the Gaelic League in 1893, an Irish nationalist body formed with the purpose of preserving traditional Irish language and culture, radically altered the cultural status of step dance.

steps.action: Frank Hall has described this as the moment in w
steps.text: Frank Hall has described this as the moment in which "step-Dancing in Ireland became 'Irish dancing'", and as therefore the most significant single event in the development of the dance form.

steps.action: Although informal competitions had long been hel
steps.text: Although informal competitions had long been held between towns and students of different dance masters, the first organised feis was held in 1897 by the League.

steps.action: The League began to codify and promote the form
steps.text: The League began to codify and promote the form of step dance which was practiced in southern areas.

steps.action: This codification, practised from the early 1920
steps.text: This codification, practised from the early 1920s, greatly narrowed the range of traditional Irish dances acceptable in popular culture.

steps.action: CLRG created certifications for dance teachers a
steps.text: CLRG created certifications for dance teachers and began to hold examinations for adjudicators of feisanna.

steps.action: In the 19th century, the Irish diaspora had spre
steps.text: In the 19th century, the Irish diaspora had spread Irish dance all over the world, especially to North America and Australia.
